content.full_text:
BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

In the Matter of

Opinion requested by
William L. Owen, City
Attorney, City of Davis

)
)
)
)
)
)

No. 76-005
June 2, 1976

BY THE COMMISSION: We have been asked the following
questions by William L. Owen, City Attorney, City of Davis:

Are planning commissioners or city council members
prohibited from making, participating in the making, or in
any way using their official position to influence the adop-
tion of a specific land use plan for the future of the City
of Davis "core area" when:

(a) A planning commissioner owns a home across the
street from one boundary of the "core area," in a neighbor-
hood which is viewed as inextricably tied to the "core area"
in terms of planning considerations. This question relates
to City of Davis Planning Commissioner Marshall Hunt.

(b) A planning commissioner is a limited partner in
a partnership which owns a vacant lot within the existing
commercial zone of the "core area" and is in the process of
constructing a commercial building thereon. This question
relates to City of Davis Planning Commissioner James A.
Willett.

1/Mr. Owen's original inquiry stated:

A planning commissioner is a limited partner in
a partnership which plans to acquire a vacant lot within
the existing commercial zone of the "core area" for
the purpose of constructing a building thereon.

In a letter to the Chairman of the Commission dated
May 5, 1976, Mr. Owen explained that the following subsequent
events had occurred:

(a) The limited partnership has acquired ownership of
the then vacant lot.
(b) Architectural plans for a commercial structure
were approved by the City and a building permit has been issued.
(c) Construction of the structure is now approximately
50 percent complete.
(d) The limited partnership has entered into leases
with commercial tenants who will take occupancy upon comple-
tion of the structure.

No. 76-005
Page Two

2 FPPC OPINIONS 78

(c) A Council member is a limited partner in a retail
business (natural foods store) which leases space within an
existing commercial building in the "core area." This question
relates to Davis City Councilman Robert N. Black.

The relevant facts set forth in the opinion request
are as follows:

The City of Davis has designated a 23 square block
area as its "core area." This area includes the traditional
"downtown" shopping area as well as a number of older residen-
tial dwellings. Many of the residential dwellings are rented
to students or senior citizens and are generally among the
lowest cost housing available in the city. The city is cur-
rently in the process of reviewing its land use plans for the
"core area." The Planning Commission and the City Council
will be required to make the significant policy decisions
regarding any "core plan." Among the possible recommenda-
tions to be included in such a plan are: expansion of com-
mercial facilities at the expense of removal of existing low
cost rental units; changes in traffic patterns by designation
of one-way streets; and an increase in parking areas. Deci-
sions on these and other possible alternatives will likely
have the effect of either preserving the "status quo" in
terms of the number and variety of commercial enterprises
within the area or allowing for significant expansion of
such development. In sum, the adoption of the "core area"
plan in its final form may have significant financial effects
on the value of holdings in real property and commercial busi-
ness establishments within and near the "core area."

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts we have been provided, we conclude
that Planning Commissioner Willett should disqualify himself
from participating in the decision to adopt the "core area"
plan. Commissioner Hunt's and Councilman Black's participation
is not barred.

ANALYSIS

We now consider individually the three questions pre-
sented. The pertinent sections of the Political Reform Act
provide:2/

87100. No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate in the

2/All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

No. 76-005
Page Three

2 FPPC OPINIONS 79

making or in any way attempt to use his official posi-
tion to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

87103. An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public offi-
cial has a direct or indirect investment worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the public offi-
cial has a direct or indirect interest worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course
of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value received by or promised to
the public official within twelve months prior to
the time when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the public offi-
cial is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management. For purposes of
this section, indirect investment or interest means
any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child of a public official, by an agent on
behalf of a public official, by any business entity
controlled by the public official or by a trust in
which he has a substantial interest. A business
entity is controlled by a public official if the pub-
lic official, his agents, spouse and dependent child-
ren hold more than fifty percent of the ownership
interest in the entity. A public official has a
substantial interest in a trust when the official,
his spouse and dependent children have a present or
future interest worth more than one thousand dollars
($1,000).

As we have previously stated in the opinion requested
by Tom Thorner, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, 202 (No. 75-089, Dec. 4,
1975):

Under the foregoing sections, several elements
must be present before a public official is required
to disqualify himself from participation in a govern-
mental decision. First, it must be reasonably

NO. 76-005
Page Four

2 FPPC OPINIONS 80

foreseeable that the governmental decision will have
a financial effect. Second, the anticipated financial
effect must be on a financial interest of the official, as defined in Sections 87103(a) through (d).
Third, the anticipated financial effect must be
material. And fourth, the governmental decision's
anticipated financial effect on the official's financial interest must be distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally.

With respect to these elements, Mr. Owen states in his
opinion request that:

The ultimate resolution of the "core area" plan
may have substantial impact upon future commercial
competition and upon property values of both residential and commercial properties.

Since the interests held by the persons concerned in
the instant matter all exceed in value the threshold amounts
specified in Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d),
supra, we interpret this statement as a stipulation that with
respect to Commissioners Hunt and Willett and Councilman Black,
each will be making decisions on the core area plan which will
foreseeably have a material financial effect upon their financial interests.3/

We accordingly turn to an analysis of whether
the effects of the decisions on the officials' financial
interests are distinguishable from an effect on the
public generally.

The Commission has recently adopted regulations concerning the meaning of the phrase "effect on the public
generally." These regulations state:

A material financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official's interests, as described
in Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d),
is distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally unless the decision will affect the official's interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect all members of the public or a
significant segment of the public....

2 Cal. Adm. Code § 18703.

_________________________________
3/This is not to say that all decisions concerning the
core area and the plan will have a material effect on these
interests. Some or most may very likely have de minimis impact
on the public officials' various interests. For further definition of what constitutes a "material financial effect," see the
Commission's regulations defining that phrase at 2 Cal. Adm.
Code § 18702.

No. 76-005
Page Five

2 FPPC OPINIONS 81

In the instant case, we interpret the phrase "public
generally" as comprising those persons within the jurisdiction
of the respective officials, i.e., the residents and persons
doing business in the City of Davis. While the adoption of
the "core area" plan may have some financial impact on the
economic interests of all residents of Davis, it can hardly
be contended that an effort to either revitalize, commercialize or otherwise seriously alter the area bounded by the "core
area" plan will affect all residents "in substantially the
same manner." Some persons or groups of persons will be economically affected more directly than others.4/ The question,
therefore, becomes what constitutes a "significant segment" of
the public.5/

(a) With respect to inquiry "(a)," we conclude that
resident home owners within and in the immediate vicinity
of the "core area" constitute a "significant segment" of the
public and that Planning Commissioner Hunt accordingly need
not disqualify himself from participation merely because he
owns a home as described. While certain aspects of the plan
may operate to increase the value of this home, either as
residential or commercial property, it is apparent that the
plan will have a "substantially similar" effect on numerous
other residential properties, perhaps throughout the entire
city as well those near the "core area." The effect
of the plan on residential property values is speculative at
this point. More importantly, there is no indication that the
plan or any portion thereof will have a peculiar impact on
the value of Commissioner Hunt's property. If further evidence
emerges in the development of the plan which would distinguish
the effect of the plan on a portion of it upon Commissioner
Hunt's interest in comparison to other residential property
owners, he may be required to disqualify himself with respect
to the matter before him for decision.

(b) We reach a contrary conclusion with respect to
inquiry "(b)." The "segment" of the "public" owning buildings
leased for commercial purposes is much smaller than that of
residential property owners. In terms of the regulation, 2
Cal. Adm. Code § 18703, supra, this segment of the general

_________________________________

4/For instance, persons who do not hold property in the
city may "benefit" from increased shopping convenience, or even
lower retail prices occasioned by increased competition. They
will not, however, stand to profit to the same degree as those
having direct or indirect financial interests in the "core area."

5/The regulations do not articulate precise circumstances
defining what is a significant segment, but rather establish certain categories of entities which may not be defined as "significant segments" of the public.

No. 76-005
Page Six

2 FPPC OPINIONE 82

public could hardly be called significant. Furthermore, the
effect of the plan will be much more direct and particular in
benefitting such persons and their interests. The benefit to
be realized by persons with interests such as those of
Mr. Willett appears to be immediate; and the decisions to be
made in adopting the "core area" plan appear crucial to the
success of the investment. For example, it seems likely that
adoption of certain proposals for the "core area" would serve
to increase the value of Commissioner Willett's property, but
could, on the other hand, serve to increase the number of
competing leased property owners and hence, the profit to be
realized by the building itself. We cannot conclude that an
effect of a decision on Commissioner Willett's investment
qualifies as an effect on any significant segment of the pub-
lic generally. Rather, aspects of the plan are likely to have
particular and identifiable effects on Commissioner Willett's
investment. Accordingly, we conclude that he is barred from
participating in decisions on those aspects of the plan which
will materially affect his investment.

The situation posed by inquiry "(c)" lies somewhere
between situations (a) and (b), above, in terms of the exist-
ence of any peculiar effect of the plan on the financial
interest of the public official. There is no doubt that the
adoption of the plan may have some economic repercussions on
the retail merchants of the city. As indicated on page 2,
supra, there is a possibility of increased competition, but
there is also the possibility of increasing numbers of poten-
tial customers shopping in the "core area" commercial estab-
lishments. There are a substantial number and variety of retail
establishments in the "core area." Approximately 250 such
enterprises currently exist, and the goods and services offered
encompass, a wide range, including restaurants, grocery stores,
specialty shops and other commercial establishments. The
provisions of the plan are likely to affect all these estab-
lishments in substantially the same way.

It is true that under our regulations a single industry,
trade or profession does not constitute the "public generally."
We do not believe retail merchants constitute a single industry,
however. Rather, such merchants constitute a major part of what
is generally regarded as the business community and taken as a
whole, may reasonably be regarded as "the public generally"
within the meaning of Section 87103 and our regulations.

There is no reason to believe that the value of Council-
man Black's interest will be affected differently from other
retail merchants. For example, there is no indication that
the plan will have a particularly beneficial (or harmful) effect
on food stores. Nor need we be concerned with any possible

No. 76-005
Page Seven

2 FPPC OPINIONS 83

effects of the plan on the value of the land on which Councilman
Black's store is located. Unlike Commissioner Willett, Councilman Black merely leases space on a rather short-term basis, and
thus the proposed changes in the character of the area will
affect only the amount of income to be derived from the store,
not the intrinsic value of the property itself. Furthermore,
there is no evidence that the adoption of the plan, or any of
its provisions, will serve to create any adverse effect on
retail businesses outside the "core area." Accordingly, the
provisions of the Political Reform Act do not appear to bar
Councilman Black's participation. Again, however, where Councilman Black's interests may be affected in a singular fashion by
an aspect of a "core area" plan decision, his participation on
this aspect may be barred.

Approved by the Commission on June 2, 1976.
Concurring: Carpenter, Japan, Quinn and Lowenstein.
Commissioner Broshahan abstained.

[Signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5931 322-6441

August 11, 1976

MR. William L. Owen, City Attorney
City of Davis
226 F Street
Davis CA 95616

Dear Mr. Owen:

This is in response to your letter of July 19, 1976, and your attached memorandum to the City Council dated July 1, 1976.

On April 21, 1976, Chairman Lowenstein responded to your letter of April 2, 1976, requesting an opinion concerning substantially the same matters raised in the instant request for advice. In that letter, it was made clear that Living Systems is a "source of income" to Councilman Kopper within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103. The Chairman's letter further stated that "it is worth noting, clear from your letter, that the decisions you list in your second question will have a 'material financial effect' on Living Systems." I interpret paragraph four of page three of your letter to imply that Living Systems may reasonably foreseeably stand to profit from these decisions. To the extent that the requirements of 'materiality' are present Councilman Kopper should not participate."

The Chairman's interpretation was based on the fact that Living Systems had been contacted by other municipalities concerning the possibility of performing work for those municipalities on a project comparable to the one proposed for the City of Davis. It was reasoned that, under these circumstances, a decision by the City Council to accept Living Systems recommendations would foreseeably have the effect of increasing the credibility of the company, thereby aiding its competitive position in the market for similar future contracts. Accordingly, the decision might have a "material financial effect" on the company.

Your memorandum of July 1, 1976, states that it is not "foreseeable" that this chain of events will transpire. It is our view that we lack sufficient facts either to endorse your

76 048 [illegible]

61103

[illegible]
62638

Mr. William Owen
August 11, 1976
Page Two


reasoning or to disagree with it. We do not disagree with the
legal reasoning you have used in arriving at your conclusion,
but we feel the following observations are relevant.

You concede that "although it is certainly possible that
a public agency might make its decision in hiring a consultant
solely or primarily upon the basis of whether their recommendations
have been adopted by the City of Davis, it is no more than speculation at this time". A great many matters are speculative in
the sense that they may not be subject to prediction with absolute
certainty. On the other hand, circumstances may be such that a
reasonable person can have a prudent expectation of the likelihood of the occurrence of certain events.

It is our belief that the answer to several questions would
affect our conclusion on the matter of foreseeability in the
situation you present. In particular: Does Living Systems
intend to advertise, or otherwise stress in attempting to
solicit future business, the acceptance of its "Draft Report"
by the City of Davis? Is it a standard practice in the awarding
of such contracts to evaluate the past work performance of the
company on similar contracts? If so, is the acceptance of such
plans by another community a criterion of such evaluation?
Are other municipalities contemplating contracting for the type
of services offered by Living Systems? Your memorandum does not
address these questions and we believe a full analysis of the
foreseeability issue requires their resolution.

With respect to matter "B" in your memorandum, we have
no reason to dispute your reasoning. We do not, however, think
that much of the reasoning is relevant to an analysis of
Councilman Roper's responsibilities under the Political Reform
Act. We are well aware that there is a great deal of misconception on the question of what constitutes a conflict of
interest under the Political Reform Act. We also quite agree
with you that a stated official careening a certain
philosophical point of view is both proper and desirable.
However, as stipulated in the Commission's regulation on
"material financial effect", 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702,
the question of whether an official knows or has reason to know
that his judgment will be clouded by his financial interest is of
paramount concern. The regulation provides in pertinent part:

(a) The financial effect of a governmental decision
on a financial interest of a public official is material
if, at the time the official makes, participates in
making or attempts to use his or her official position
to influence the making of the decision, in light of

Mr. William Owen
August 11, 1976
Page Three

all the circumstances and facts known at the time
of the decision, the official knows or has reason to
know that the existence of the financial interest might
interfere with the official's performance of his or her
duties in an impartial manner free from bias.

(b) In determining the existence of a material effect
upon a financial interest, consideration should be given,
but not be limited to, an analysis of the following
factors: ...

(3) In the case of a source of income of a
public official as defined in Government Code
Section 87103(c):

(A) The decision will affect the source
of income in the manner described in subsection (b)(1)
above;

(B) Whether the governmental decision will
directly affect the amount of income to be received
by the official;

(C) Whether there is a nexus between the
governmental decision and the purpose for which the
official received income.

The specific dollar or percentage amounts set
forth above do not constitute either absolute maximum
or minimum levels, but are merely intended to provide
guidance and should be considered along with other
relevant factors in determining whether a financial
interest may interfere with the official's exercise
of his or her duties in rendering a decision....

In light of your apparent disagreement with the Chair-
man's conclusion that there is a "just published question"
as to a conflict of interest in Councilman Kopper's situation,
[illegible] [illegible] [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]
if you agree, I would appreciate your submitting a report
in order to have this matter resolved by the Commission.1/

1/ There is another issue which possibly may be addressed
in such an opinion concerning Councilman Kopper's participation, to wit:

Is it foreseeable that Councilman Kopper might be biased
in voting upon the rents plan, considering his original
participation in the preparation of that plan as Sunbelt
Savings & Loan's officer, a claim of all then do a not.

William Owen
August 11, 1976
Page Four


If you do not wish to resubmit your request, it should be
clear that the staff neither endorses or disavows the conclusion in your memorandum.  This letter, however, will
not serve to immunize Councilman Kopper from an enforcement
proceeding under the Political Reform Act.

If you have any further questions, please contact
Mr. Kenneth Goshorn.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

KG:pvp




(footnote continued from previous page)

        valuable employee (consultant) to Living Systems
        and hence might expect to receive increased duties
        or assignments carrying commensurately larger
        remuneration to him personally?  Or, more generally,
        would he, by enhancing Living Systems' marketability
        as a consulting firm, be enhancing his own potential
        for future consultative work?

CITY OF DAVIS
226 F Street Davis, California 95616
(916) 756-3740

July 19, 1976

Kenneth W. Goshorn, Esq.
Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Goshorn:

I am enclosing a copy of a draft opinion which I prepared at the request of the Davis City Council. This draft is being submitted to you for consideration pursuant to 2 California Administrative Code Section 18329.

I look forward to receiving your written advice of the Commission in accordance with said Administrative regulation.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
WILLIAM L. OWEN
City Attorney

WLO:pb

enclosure

July 1, 1976


MEMORANDUM TO:  City Council

FROM        :  City Attorney

SUBJECT     :  Neighborhood Planning - Proposed
               Energy Conservation Amendments


                    QUESTION PRESENTED

     You have asked for my opinion on the following:  May
Councilman Kopper participate in the consideration of proposed energy
conservation amendments to the City's zoning, subdivision and community
design standards?

                         OPINION

     It is my opinion that Councilman Kopper is not disqualified
from considering the adoption of such amendments.

                         FACTS

     On April 2, 1976, at Councilman Kopper's request, an opinion
was sought from the Fair Political Practices Commission concerning his
ability to vote on proposed revisions to the City's zoning, subdivision
and community design regulations.  On April 21, 1976, the Commission
Chairman responded, stating that the request ". . . raises no substantial
questions involving an interpretation of the [Political Reform] Act".
The letter went on to point out that the "Act" requires disqualification
where the official action foreseeably will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, upon
a source of income.

     In the instant case, the City of Davis contracted with several
individual consultants in 1973 for the purpose of obtaining recommenda-
tions for energy conservation measures.  In 1974 the consultants
published "A Strategy for Energy Conservation" which contained
recommendations for changes in the City's existing street width and
parking standards, modification of street tree planting policies,
revision of subdivision and lot design policies and other measures
calculated to produce energy savings in new neighborhoods.  Such recom-
mendations, if adopted by the City Council, would apply to all future
streets, street trees, subdivisions and developments.

     Councilman Kopper has advised me that he participated in the
research and writing of a portion of "Strategy" under a subcontract with
the authors.  In 1973 he was paid approximately $700 for such work.

City Council
July 1, 1976
Page 2


     Subsequent to the submission of "Strategy", two of the authors
formed the business known as Living Systems. This business has offices
in Yolo County, and Councilman Kopper has been an employee of Living
Systems since October 1975. Prior to October 1975, he received no
compensation from the authors of "Strategy" except for the original $700
figure.

     Since October 1975, Mr. Kopper has been engaged in various
work for Living Systems, including some text editing of a document
entitled "Planning for Energy Conservation - Draft Report". This report
recommend various specific ordinances and resolutions to implement the
programs suggested in the "Strategy". The draft report was funded by a
HUD grant to the City of Davis and was carried out through a contract
(approved in September 1975) between the City and Living Systems.

                              ANALYSIS

     A.  Reasonable Foreseeability.

          Initially, it must be determined whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the official action (approval or disapproval of the
proposed revisions to the City's zoning, subdivision and community design
standards) will have a material effect upon Councilman Kopper's source of
income (Living Systems).

          Reasonable foreseeability of financial effect requires more
than a mere "possibility". Rather, a "substantial probability" of
financial benefit has been referred to by the courts in conflict of
interest cases. United States v. Mississippi Valley Generating Company
(1961) 364 U.S. 520, 535.

          It should be noted that the Fair Political Practices
Commission has determined that a water district director was not
disqualified from voting on the lifting of a water connection moratorium
even though the director's husband was an employee of a large
construction company which had previously been engaged in construction of
a substantial project within the district. The Commission concluded
that it was not reasonably foreseeable that the lifting of the water
connection moratorium would have a material financial effect on the
construction company or the husband's salary where the company had only
carried out one large project within the district during the previous
ten-year period. (1 FPPC Opinions 198, 208, 209.)

          In the present case, the sole possibility of financial
effect upon Living Systems seems to be that if Davis adopts the
suggested general energy conservation amendments, the authors of the

City Council
July 1, 1976
Page 3


"Strategy" and the "Draft Report" may be given greater standing by other
communities who are desirous of hiring energy consultants. This argument
postulates that the official action of adopting or rejecting the proposed
amendments could enhance Living Systems' professional reputation which
may affect the firm's ability to compete with other consulting firms for
public agency business. 1/

Although it is certainly possible that a public agency
might make its decision in hiring a consultant solely or primarily upon
the basis of whether their recommendations have been adopted by the City
of Davis, it is no more than speculation at this time. In fact, it is
well known that decisions on the hiring of consultants are complex and
involve numerous considerations including the cost of services, the time
estimates for completion, the educational and experience backgrounds of
the principals, and numerous other factors. To postulate that a future
contract between another public agency and Living Systems will be
dependent upon the adoption or rejection by Davis of the proposed zoning,
subdivision and community design amendments is extremely speculative and
does not meet the "reasonably foreseeable" nor "Substantial probability"
tests of the Political Reform Act.

B.  Impartiality.

Councilman Kopper has publicly stated his support for the
general proposition of local government involvement in energy conservation. Persons who disagree with such position may well assert a
"conflict of interest" based upon his announced position. It is my
opinion that any such assertion of conflict would be unjustified.

It should be noted that the public conception of "conflict
of interest" is often not in conformity with the legal proscriptions.
As noted above, the legal parameters of conflicts relate to reasonably
foreseeable financial gain from official decisions. Although judges and
local legislators, when acting in a quasi-judicial proceeding, are
required to be impartial, no such judicial non-involvement is required
of a local official when acting in a legislative capacity on a matter of
general concern. City of Fairfield v. Superior Court (1975) 14 C.3d 768.

It is clear that the proposed amendments to the City's
general zoning, subdivision and community design regulations are
legislative in character.

_________________________

1/  By copy of memorandum opinion dated March 18, 1976, Councilman Kopper was
advised that the City of Davis could not enter into any new agreements
or amendments to preexisting agreements with Living Systems so long as
he is both a member of the City Council and an employee of Living
Systems.

City Council
July 1, 1976
Page 4


In the Fairfield, supra., case, a real estate developer
attempted to disqualify two city councilmen from voting on his shopping
center proposal based upon their publicly announced opposition to the
project. The California Supreme Court rejected the argument that the
councilmen were disqualified, as follows:

"A councilman has not only a right but an obligation to discuss issues of vital concern with his
constituents and to state his views on matters of
public importance. His role in the community is
depicted in Todd v. City of Visalia (1967) 254
Cal.App.2d 679 [62 Cal.Rptr. 485]. Rejecting a
contention that councilmen who had acquired
information outside the hearing room concerning a
proposed assessment district were disqualified
from voting at a hearing to determine the validity
of that district, the court observed that: 'A
city councilman is elected usually because of his
acquaintanceship and popularity. He may not be
instructed on many of the technical matters to which
he is called upon to pass judgment. He is frequently
an extrovert, who circulates widely in the community
and talks with businessmen and voters about all
sorts of questions that may come before the council.
He is a legislator, an administrator, and at times a
quasi-judicial officer. In the present instance,
it would be strange if the members of the council
living and working in Visalia did not have
considerable cognizance of what was going on in
the city, of the efforts of some people to form the
present assessment district and of the municipal
needs in this respect, fanciful or actual.'
(254 Cal.App.2d at p. 691.)

"Many of the alleged statements on these community
matters that plaintiffs seek to adduce were made
during the election campaign of Campos and Cummins.
Campaign statements, however, do not disqualify the
candidate from voting on matters which come before
him after his election. In Wollen v. Fort Lee
(1958) 27 N.J. 408 [142 A.2d 881], the court stated:
'[I]t would be contrary to the basic principles of a

City Council
July 1, 1976
Page 5


"Free society to disqualify from service in the
popular assembly those who had made pre-election
commitments of policy on issues involved in the
performance of their sworn . . . duties. Such is
not the bias or prejudice upon which the law looks
askance. The contrary rule of action
would frustrate freedom of expression for the
enlightenment of the electorate that is of the
very essence of our democratic society.' (142
A.2d at p. 888; see City of Farmer's Branch v.
Hawnco., Inc. (Tex.Civ.App. 1968) 435 S.W.2d 288,
292; Kramer v. Bd. of Adjust., Sea Girt (1965)
45 N.J. 268 [212 A.2d 153, 160-161]; Binford v.
Western Electric Company (1963) 219 Ca. 404
[133 S.E.2d 361, 364]; cf. Montana Power Co. v.
Public Service Commission (D.Mont. 1935) 12
F.Supp. 946.)

"In Moscow v. Boston Redevelopment Authority (1965)
349 Mass. 553 [210 N.E.2d 699], reviewing a city
council's determination to exclude certain property
from a redevelopment plan, the Massachusetts
Supreme Judicial Court ruled that: 'No member of
the city council was disqualified because he had
expressed an opinion or taken sides on the merits
of the Plan including Parcel 8 whether during an
election campaign or at any other time. . . . The
issue was wholly political in character. . . . It
was likewise one of great interest in the
community, and the voters were entitled to know the
views of the candidates, who had a right, and per-
haps a duty, to state their positions.' (210 N.E.2d
at p. 707.)" City of Fairfield v. Superior Court
(1975) 14 C.3d 768, 780-781.

CONCLUSION

For the foregoing reasons, it is my opinion that Councilman
Kopper is not disqualified from the consideration of the proposed
amendments to the City's zoning, subdivision and community design
standards.


WLO:pb                                WILLIAM L. OWEN
                                      City Attorney

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95801 • • • 1160 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                           322-5931                    322-6441

April 21, 1976

Mr. William L. Owen
City Attorney
226 F Street
Davis, CA. 95616

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-048

Dear Mr. Owen:

Thank you for your letter dated April 2, 1976, requesting
the Fair Political Practices Commission to issue an opinion con-
cerning possible conflicts of interest. Since your letter
raises no substantial questions involving an interpretation of
the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the
following informal comments will be helpful.

In response to your first question, the Political Reform
Act does not prevent the making of a contract.1/ Rather, the
question is the lawfulness of the participation by Councilman
Kopper in governmental decisions affecting Living Systems, an
employer of the Councilman.

Section 87103 defines "financial interest" to include:

. . . (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course
of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value received by or promised to
the public official within twelve months prior to the
time when the decision is made; . . . .

From the facts, you state it is apparent that Living
Systems is a "source of income" to Councilman Kopper. Further-
more, his immediate resignation from the employ of Living Systems
would not change this result since for the next twelve months
the company would continue to constitute a financial interest
under the provisions of the Political Reform Act.

_____________
1/     We note that you have advised Mr. Kopper concerning the
provisions of Gov. Code §1090. Our response is specifically
limited to an analysis of the provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

Mr. William L. Owen
April 21, 1976
Page Two


The Commission recently has adopted an emergency
regulation concerning "source of income" which states:

     Source of income, as used in Government Code Section
87103(c), shall not include a former employer if:
All income from the employer was received by or accrued
to the public official prior to the time he or she
became a public official; the income was received in
the normal course of the previous employment; and
there was no expectation by the public of at the
time he or she assumed office of renewed employment
with the former employer.

                                    2 Cal. Adm. Code §18704

It does not appear that this regulation is applicable to the
facts surrounding Councilman Kopper's employment. He did not
receive all income "prior to the time he became a public
official." Accordingly, Mr. Kopper should disqualify himself
from participating in decisions which "it is reasonably fore-
seeable ... will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
able from its effect on the public generally," on Living
Systems.

It is not entirely clear from your letter whether the
decisions you list in your second question will have a
"material financial effect" on Living Systems. I interpret
paragraph four on page three of your letter to imply that
Living Systems may reasonably foreseeably stand to profit from
these decisions. To the extent that the requirements of
"materiality" are present, Councilman Kopper should not participate.
See generally, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702, "Material Financial
Effect," as it relates to a "source of income."

I trust that the foregoing analysis will be of assistance
to you in analyzing the referenced matter. If you have any
further questions, please contact Kenneth Goshorn, an Attorney
in the Conflicts of Interest Division at (916) 322-6441.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   [signature]
                                   Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                   Chairman
DHL:pvp

CITY OF DAVIS
226 F Street    Davis, California 95616
(916) 756-3740

April 2, 1976

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1150 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Opinion Regarding
     Conflict of Interest Provisions

Dear Chairman Lowenstein:

I hereby request, on behalf of Davis City Councilman,
William D. Kopper, an official opinion concerning the following
questions relating to the Political Reform Act of 1974:

1. Is a contract for consulting services permissible
   under Article 1 of Chapter 7 of the Political
   Reform Act of 1974, where a member of the city
   council permanently resigns his employment posi-
   tion with the consulting firm prior to submission
   of the consulting services proposal and where
   such councilman abstains from all discussion and
   action on such proposal?

2. May a councilman vote on revisions to zoning,
   subdivision and community design regulations
   based upon recommendations prepared by a consult-
   ing firm which presently employs the councilman?

3. If the response to question No. 2 is negative,
   would the response be different should the
   councilman permanently resign his employment prior
   to consideration of the proposed revisions?

FACTS (QUESTION NO. 1)

On March 2, 1976, William D. Kopper was elected to the Davis
City Council. Mr. Kopper is an environmental consultant.

sections.analysis:
We now consider individua}_ly the three questions pre-
sented. 
The pertinent sections of the Politicai Reform Act 
provide:~/ 
87100. 
No public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate in the 
~/All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 


No. 76-005 
Page Three 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 79 
making or in any way attempt to use his official posi-
tion to influence a governmental decision in \vhich he 
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. 
87103. 
An official has a financial interest in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishab:e from its 
effect on the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public offi-
cial has a direct or indirect investment \vorth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000) i 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public offi-
cial has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($lrOOO) ~ 
(c) 
Any sou~ce of income, other than loans by a 
coIllttlercial lending institution in the regular course 
of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars 
($250) or more in value received by or promised to 
the public official within twelve months prior to 
the time when the decision is madA~ or 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public offi-
cial is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management. 
For purposes of 
this section, indirect investment or interest means 
any investment or interest mmed by the spouse or 
dependent child of a public official, by an agent on 
behalf of a public official, by any business entity 
cont~olled by the public official or by a trust in 
which he has a substantial interest. 
A business 
entity js controlled by a public official if the pub-
lic official, his agents, spouse and dependent child-
ren hold more than fifty percent of the ownership 
interest in the entity. 
A public official has a 
substantial interest in a trust when the official, 
his spouse and dependent children have a present or 
future interest worth more than one thousand dollars 
($1,000) . 
As we have previously stated in the opinion requested 
by Tom Thorner, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, 202 (No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 
1975) : 
Under the foregoing sections, several elements 
must be present before a public official is required 
to disqualify himself from participation in a govern-
mental decision. 
First, it must be reasonab 


No. 76-005 
Page Four 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 80 
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have 
a financial effect. 
Second, the anticipated financial 
effect must be on a financial interest of the offi-
cial, as defined in Sections 87103(a) through (d). 
Third, the anticipated fin2ncial effect must be 
materi31. 
And. fourth, the governmental decision's 
anticipated fj.nancial effect on the official's finan-
cial interest must be distinguishable from its effect 
on the public generally. 
With respect to these elements, Mr. Owen states in his 
opinion request that: 
The ultimate resolution of the "core area" plan 
may have substantial impact upon future commercial 
competition and upon property values of both residen-
tial and commercial properties. 
Since the interests held by the persons concerned in 
the instant matter all exceed in value the threshold amounts 
specified in Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d), 
supra, we interpret this statement as a stipulation that with 
respect to Commissioners Hunt and Willett and Councilman Black, 
each will be making uecis1.ons on the core cirea plctl1 wJ.1ich will 
foreseeably have a material financial effect upon their finan-
cial interests.il 
We accordingly turn to an analysis of whether 
the effects of the decisions on the officials' financial 
interests are distinguishable from an effect on the 
public generally. 
The Commission has recently adopted regulations con-
cerning the meaning of the phrase "effect on the public 
generally." 
These regulations state: 
A material financial effect of a governmental 
decision on an official's interests, as described 
in Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d), 
is distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally unless the decision will affect the offi-
cial's interest in substantially the same manner 
as it will affect all members of the public or a 
significant segment of the public .... 
2 Cal. Adm. Code § 18703. 
ilThis is not to say that all decisions concerning the 
core area and the plan will have a material effect on these 
interests. 
Some or most may very likely have de minimis impact 
on the public officials' various interests. 
For further defini-
tion of what constitutes a "material financial effect," see the 
Commission's regulations defining that phrase at 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code § 18702. 


No. 76-005 
Page Five 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 81 
In the instant case, we interpret the phrase "public 
generally" as comprising those persons within the jurisdiction 
of the respective officials, i.e., the residents and persons 
doing bus 
s in the City of Davis. 
While the adoption of 
the "core area" plan may have some financial 
act on the 
economic interests of all residents of Davis, it can hardly 
be contendc:d that an effort to either revitalize r cornmercial-
ize or othcr\'lisc scriollsJ:l cllte~c tILe. dr·ect 1)O-ullC1e(1 1:'}7 
tJ"!.(-::; "cor2 
area 1\ plan v;rill affect all residents 
II in substantially the 
same manner." 
Some persons or groups of persons will be eco-
nomically affect.ed more directly than others. il The question, 
therefore 1 becomes what cons·ti tutes a "significant segment" of 
the public.~1 
(a) 
With respect to inquiry n{a)," we conclude that 
residential home 0'm1ers within and in the immediate vicinity 
of the "core areal! cons 
tutue a "significant segment" of the 
public and that Planning Commissioner Hunt accordingly need 
not di 
ify himself from participation merely because he 
owns a home as described. 
While certain aspects of the plan 
may operate ·to increase the value of this home I either as 
residential or commercial property lit is apparent: that the 
plan will have a "substantially similar" effect on numerous 
other residential properties, perhaps throughout the entire 
city as well those near the "core area." 
The effect 
of the plan on residential property values is speculative at 
this point. 
More importantly, there is no indication that the 
plan or 
any portion thereof will have a peculiar impact on 
the value of Corrnnissioner Hunt's property. 
If further evidence 
emerges in the development of the plan which would distinguish 
the effect of the plan or a portion of it upon Commissioner 
Hunt's interest in comparison to other residential property 
owners, he may be required to disqualify himself with respect 
to the matter before him for decision. 
(b) 
We reach a contrary conclusion with respect to 
inquiry" (b) . 1/ 
The "segment." of the "public" owning buildings 
leased for commercial purposes is much smaller than that of 
residential property owners. 
In terms of the regulation, 2 
Cal. Adm. Code § 18703, supra, this segment of the general 
i/For instance, persons who do not hold property in the 
city may "benefit" from increased shopping convenience, or even 
10vler retail prices occasioned by increased competi tion. 
They 
v;rill not I hmvever, stand to profit to the same degree as those 
having direct or indirect financial interests in the "core area." 
~/The regulations do not articulate precise circumstances 
defining what is a significant segment, but rather establish cer-
tain categorieS-of entities which may not be defined as "signifi-
cant segments" of the public. 


No. 76-005 
Page Six 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 82 
public could hardly be called significant. 
Furthermore, the 
effect of the plan will be much more direct and particular in 
benefit±ing such persons and their interests. 
The benefit to 
be realized by persons \vith interests such as those of 
Mr. Willett appears to be immediatei and the decisions to be 
made in adopting the "core area" plan appear crucial to the 
success of the investment. 
For example, it seems likely that 
adoption of certain proposals for the 
I! core area" would serve 
to increase the value of Commissioner %fillett' s property, but 
could, on the other hand, serve to increase the number of 
competing leased property owners and hence, the profit to be 
realized by the building itself. 
We cannot conclude that an 
effect of a decision on Commissioner Willett's investment 
qualifies as an effect on any significant segment of the pub-
lic generally. 
Rather, aspects of the plan are likely to have 
particular and i.dentifiable effects on Corrt..'l1issioner vlillett f s 
investment. 
Accordingly, we conclude that he is barred from 
participating in decisions on those aspects of the plan which 
will materially affect his investment. 
The situation posed by inquiry "(c)" lies somewhere 
between situations (a) and (b) f 
above, in terms of the exist-
ence of any peculiar effect of the plan. on the financial 
i~terest of the public official. 
There is no doubt that the 
adoption of the plan may have some economic repercussions on 
the retail merchants of the city. 
As indicated on page 2, 
supra, there is a possibility of increased competition, but 
there is also the possibility of increasing numbers of poten-
tial customers shopping in the "core area" commercial estab-
lishments. 
There are a substantial number and variety of retail 
establishments in the "core area." 
Approximately 250 such 
enterprises currently exist, and the goods and services offered 
encompass, a wide range, including restaurants, grocery stores, 
specialty. shops and other commercial establishments. 
The 
provisions of the plan are likely to affect all these estab-
lishments in substantially the same way. 
It is true that under our regulations a single industry, 
trade or profession does not constitute the "public generally." 
We do not believe retail merchants constitute a single industry, 
however. 
Rather, such merchants constitute a major part of what 
is generally regarded as the business community and taken as a 
whole, may reasonably be regarded as "the public generally" 
within the meaning of Section 87103 and our regulations. 
There is no reason to believe that the value of Council-
man Black's interest will be affected differently from other 
retail merchants. 
For example, there is no indication that 
the plan will have a particularly beneficial (or harmful) effect 
on food stores. 
Nor need we be concerned with any possible 


No. 76-005 
Page Seven 
2 FPPC OPINIONS 83 
effects of the plan on the value of the land on which Councilman 
Black's store is located. 
Unlike Cowmissioner Willett, Council-
man Black merely leases space on a rather short-term basis, and 
thus the proposed changes in the character of the area will 
affect only the amount of income to be derived from the store, 
not the intrinsic value of the prope~ty itself. 
FurtherL~re, 
there is no evidence that the adoption of the plan! or any of 
its provisions, will serve to create any adverse effect on 
retail businesses outs 
the" core area." 
l-\ccording ly, the 
provisions of the Political Reform Act do not appear to bar 
Councilman Black's participation. 
Again, however, where Council-
man Black's interests may be affected in a singular fashion by 
an aspect of a "core area" plan decision, his participation on 
this aspect may be barred. 
Approved by the Commission on June 2, 1976. 
Concurring: 
Carpenter, Lapan, Quinn and Lowenstein. 
ConmLissioner Brosnahan abstained. 
g/Lg~~,~~_ 
Daniel H. Lowenstein 
Chairman

yours,

embedding.first_500_words: BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION In the Matter of Opinion requested by William L. Owen, City Attorney, City of Davis ) ) ) ) ) ) No. 76-005 June 2, 1976 BY THE COMMISSION: We have been asked the following questions by William L. Owen, City Attorney, City of Davis: Are planning commissioners or city council members prohibited from making, participating in the making, or in any way using their official position to influence the adop- tion of a specific land use plan for the future of the City of Davis "core area" when: (a) A planning commissioner owns a home across the street from one boundary of the "core area," in a neighbor- hood which is viewed as inextricably tied to the "core area" in terms of planning considerations. This question relates to City of Davis Planning Commissioner Marshall Hunt. (b) A planning commissioner is a limited partner in a partnership which owns a vacant lot within the existing commercial zone of the "core area" and is in the process of constructing a commercial building thereon. This question relates to City of Davis Planning Commissioner James A. Willett. 1/Mr. Owen's original inquiry stated: A planning commissioner is a limited partner in a partnership which plans to acquire a vacant lot within the existing commercial zone of the "core area" for the purpose of constructing a building thereon. In a letter to the Chairman of the Commission dated May 5, 1976, Mr. Owen explained that the following subsequent events had occurred: (a) The limited partnership has acquired ownership of the then vacant lot. (b) Architectural plans for a commercial structure were approved by the City and a building permit has been issued. (c) Construction of the structure is now approximately 50 percent complete. (d) The limited partnership has entered into leases with commercial tenants who will take occupancy upon comple- tion of the structure. No. 76-005 Page Two 2 FPPC OPINIONS 78 (c) A Council member is a limited partner in a retail business (natural foods store) which leases space within an existing commercial building in the "core area." This question relates to Davis City Councilman Robert N. Black. The relevant facts set forth in the opinion request are as follows: The City of Davis has designated a 23 square block area as its "core area." This area includes the traditional "downtown" shopping area as well as a number of older residen- tial dwellings. Many of the residential dwellings are rented to students or senior citizens and are generally among the lowest cost housing available in the city. The city is cur- rently in the process of reviewing its land use plans for the "core area." The Planning Commission and the City Council will be required to make the significant policy decisions regarding any "core plan." Among the possible recommenda- tions to be included in such a plan are: expansion of com- mercial facilities at the expense of removal of existing low cost rental units; changes in traffic patterns
